name: B.S. in Engineering Technology, Biomedical Engineering Technology

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The plan builds three complementary spines to a device-technology outcome: a biomedical core (BME 111 Physiology -> BME 210 Biological Signal Processing -> BMET 320 Data Acquisition/Imaging -> BMET 415 Biomedical Mechatronics -> BMET 450 Senior Project, plus BMET 440 experiential), an electronics track (ECET 201 Circuits -> ECET 329 Analog/Digital Electronics), and a manufacturing/quality track (MNET 215 Materials, MNET 315 Statistics -> 416 Scheduling / 420 Quality). Math/physics/chemistry foundations and MET 303/304/403 thermo-fluids support them. Progression (depth 0->6) is smooth and appropriate for a technology (versus engineering) degree.
dimensions.suggestion: Consolidate the eight open Technical/Free Elective slots into a named concentration (e.g., medical imaging or device manufacturing) so the upper-level plan reads as intentional depth.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is healthy (deepest chain 6, BMET 450 <- BMET 415 and <- MNET 416) with rich compound conditions (BMET 320 requires a CAD course, a programming course, BME 111, physics and calculus). The 7 dangling references (CHEM 125A/126A, ENGR 211, GER 200/300, PHYS 102A/103A) are lab codes, a professional-skills seminar and gen-ed placeholders, not genuine breaks.
dimensions.suggestion: Resolve the CHEM/PHYS lab codes and ENGR 211 and GER placeholders so the metrics show zero dangling references.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives are present in the data; only the plan and course descriptions appear. For an accredited-track engineering-technology B.S. this is a real omission.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish explicit student outcomes (device instrumentation and data acquisition, quality/regulatory practice, teamwork/communication) to support accreditation.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan reconciles exactly (summedTermCredits 120 = statedTotalCredits 120, delta 0.0). Most terms are loaded at 15-16 credits, though the final term is light at 12. The distribution is broadly balanced across foundations, BME core, electronics and manufacturing, but a large share of upper-level credit is open electives (five Technical + three Free Elective slots).
dimensions.suggestion: Rebalance the light 12-credit final term and convert some of the eight open elective slots into required device/imaging courses so the major core is more fully specified.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Terms are labeled and course descriptions are complete, but the plan carries many unlabeled 'None' rows (Technical Elective 1-5, Free Elective 1-3, GER placeholders) and a fragmentary 'or EPS 202' alternative row, which leaves several requirement rows under-specified.
dimensions.suggestion: Give every plan row a code or an explicit named placeholder and render the 'ECON 201 or EPS 202' choice as a single labeled requirement.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The curriculum is current for biomedical device technology: imaging and data acquisition (BMET 320 covering MRI/ultrasound/X-ray), biomedical mechatronics (BMET 415), biological signal processing (BME 210), quality systems and ISO (MNET 420), and a required experiential course (BMET 440). The chief gaps are medical-device regulatory affairs (FDA QSR / ISO 13485), device cybersecurity, and biomaterials/sterilization.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a medical-device regulatory-affairs and quality course (FDA design controls, ISO 13485) and adopt NJIT's MIT 231 medical-device cybersecurity course.

descriptionQuality.justification: All 30 sampled courses have descriptions and the biomedical entries are specific (BMET 320 on imaging modalities and physiologic sensors; BME 210 on filtering and processing of biologically inspired signals; BMET 415 on design and control of mechatronic systems for biomedical applications). Manufacturing and foundation courses are equally clear.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are explicit with grade conditions and coherent compound logic (BMET 450 <- 'BMET 415, COM 313, and MNET 416'; ECET 329 <- ECET 201). Only the lab/seminar/GER placeholders are missing, and the chain depth is consistent with the plan.

missingTopics.topic: Medical-device regulatory affairs and quality systems (FDA QSR, ISO 13485, design controls)
missingTopics.rationale: MNET 420 teaches general quality/ISO but not medical-device-specific regulation, which is central to the field.
BMET 320 handles data acquisition with no security content; NJIT's MIT 231 (Intro to Computer Security: Medical Devices) directly fills this gap.
MNET 215 Materials and Processes is general manufacturing; biomaterials, biocompatibility and sterilization are not covered.
BMET 415 mechatronics touches control but a dedicated embedded-systems course (e.g., ECET 311) would strengthen device design.
Beyond BME 210, there is no course on programming for biomedical signal/image analysis (Python, MATLAB) that modern device work requires.

emergingTrends.trend: Connected/IoT medical devices and device cybersecurity
emergingTrends.rationale: Networked devices dominate new products, making security and interoperability essential skills that BMET 320 could extend.

emergingTrends.trend: AI/ML in medical imaging and diagnostics
emergingTrends.rationale: BMET 320 and BME 210 are natural hosts for machine-learning-based image and signal analysis.
Wearable sensors and telehealth hardware are a fast-growing device category not represented in the core.
MNET 215 could incorporate 3D printing of biomedical components and custom instrumentation.
Interoperability standards (HL7/FHIR) and SaMD are reshaping the industry and align with a data-acquisition core.

topSuggestions: Publish explicit program learning outcomes / student outcomes for accreditation.
Add medical-device regulatory affairs/quality (FDA QSR, ISO 13485) and device cybersecurity (adopt MIT 231).
Add biomaterials/biocompatibility and an embedded-systems option for device design.
Convert several of the eight open Technical/Free Elective slots into a named imaging or device-manufacturing concentration and rebalance the 12-credit final term.
Resolve the lab/seminar/GER placeholder references (CHEM 125A/126A, ENGR 211, PHYS 102A/103A, GER 200/300).
